course_title: Advanced Special Topics in German Studies
course_description:
The course develops advanced skills in German studies, including literary appreciation, film analysis and the critical interrogation of important historical movements and philosophical directions within Germany's cultural legacy. The German-language text corpus (narrative, literary, filmic) is sufficiently flexible to cater for differing students' interests and priorities.

Through a series of guided readings and seminar-style engagement with themes and issues, theďľ course aims to develop understanding of German culture, history and literature. It does so by shining a light on the time of Goethe, a period which saw the greatest flowering of thought and literary expression in the history of the German nation. The course argues that Germany today cannot be fully understood without a basic comprehension of this key period and its development. The course has three parts, each of which will be individually assessed.ďľ In the first part, we will read some ofďľ Goethe's poetry, particularly that drawn fromďľ the movement of "Sturm und Drang" ("Storm and Stress") to which Goethe was a major contributor.ďľ In the second part of the course, the story Der blonde Eckbert by Ludwig Tieck, one of the most important representatives of German Romanticism, will be the focus. In the third and finalďľ part, the course will consider Heinrich von Kleist, the greatest writer of short stories in the German tongue. A close reading of Kleist's famous story Das Erdbeben in Chili, servingďľ as a review of key ideas that emerged during theďľ "Goethezeit",ďľ will be undertaken. Regular attendance, evidence of thorough preparation and a willingness to interact in small-group discussion are essential for the successful completion of this course.

Class allocation:
In order to optimise the student experience, it may be necessary to reallocate students to a different class from their first choice.
Before this happens, every effort will be made to enable students to voluntarily change into an alternative class that is suitable.
Please note: Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system to help with class allocation. Therefore, should you need help with your timetable and/or allocation of classes, please ensure you email

the Course Code

assessment_summary.title: In-class Presentation
assessment_summary.due_date: 11/08/2025 - 25/08/2025; 3/09/2025; WEEK 3 - WEEK 5 - In-class presentations; WEEK 6 ( Wed, 5pm) - 250 word summary in German submission

assessment_details.title: In-class Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 11/08/2025 - 25/08/2025 3/09/2025 WEEK 3 - WEEK 5 - In-class presentations WEEK 6 ( Wed, 5pm) - 250 word summary in German submission
assessment_details.task_description: This component requires a 10 minute in-class oral presentation in German, to be written up as a 250-word written summary (also in German) after the presentation. Both the presentation and the summary are to focus on one poem by Goethe only . In the oral part of the presentation, you may refer to notes, but do not merely read them out aloud. Your presentation (and the subsequent write-up of the presentation) should answer directly the four questions that appear below: Wann hat Goethe dieses Gedicht geschrieben? Wo/wann/in welchem Umfeld/Kontext spielt das Gedicht? Was thematisiert das Gedicht? Was mĂ¶chte der Dichter mit dem Gedicht sagen? The 10 minute presentation is to be completed in class between WEEK 3 and WEEK 5. Students may present a maximum of 10 powerpoint slides during the presentation. 250 word summary in German is to be submitted no later than 5 pm on September 3, 2025 and is to be submitted via Turnitin. No bibliography is required, but a brief list of references may be included if you have referred to one or more secondary readings in your presentation. The grading criteria for the presentation and summary can be found in the attachment GRMN3330 Presentation Write-Up Rubric. Sign up for your presentation in class after consulting the lecturer. AI and MT Statement For the oral, in-person component, the use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
In-person presentations
Written summary submission via Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: In-Class Written Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The exam will be conducted in-class in WEEK 9 and will last up to 90 minutes. What is involved? The exam will have two parts. The first part of the exam will focus on questions relating to the narrative plot. These questions are to be answered in German. The second part of the exam will focus on questions of interpretation. Students may answer this part of the exam in English or German. No bibliography for this task is required. AI and MT Statement This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Research Essay
assessment_details.task_description: A research essay of 2000 words (excluding the bibliography) to be written in either German or English requires you to discuss and critique in-depth the topics covered during weeks 10-13 of the course in relation to Kleist's story Das Erdbeben in Chili . Questions will be made available by week 11. The essay is due on November 21, 2025 at 5 pm. In the research essay you are asked to demonstrate the capacity to marshal a wide-ranging argument and to explain it competently to a critical reader. Formal conventions governing the presentation of an academic essay are of value in this assessment task. Students are to use MLA style and to take account of the criteria below. The essay should be submitted via Blackboard. AI and MT Statement This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
